sentences.text: Quite a few were arrested by the Germans and ended up in concentration camps.
And a few didn't even come back.
They're very serious.
Yeah.
They-- when they found out that they were informers, a couple of guys, they invite them to come to a-- in a farm.
They say there was some American pilots hiding.
And when they were there, they shot them.
One was killed.
The other-- the other was injured.
And then the third one fled, and went betraying to the Germans, and tell them what happened.
No.
It was the time I was-- I was already in-- in prison.
I heard about it, yes.
My friend, Eric Van [?
Assan, ?] he was an elder in our church.
And he was an auditor with the income tax department.
He knew quite a few Jewish people, businesspeople.
And one day he approached me.
And because I was a traveling salesman I had a pass on the train.
He can go wherever you want.
And he asked me if I was able to accompany a Jewish couple to his sister-in-law in Apeldoorn, where they were going to be in hiding.
Apeldoorn is A-P-E-L-D-O-O-R-N.
It's in the center of Holland.
So I did that.
I accompanied them, and drive them to his family.
And afterward he came back.
He [? post ?] me again.
And he said, well, I have a friend who is an inspector of police force in Maastricht police office.
And the police office has a Jewish family-- they were a new family-- a widow with two teenage kids.
And [INAUDIBLE] look them for a hiding place.
And he asked me I could find a hiding place.
And it was-- the name of the family was Freilich, F-R-E-I-L-I-C-H.
And the mother, she was a widow.
And the daughter, Toni, and the son, Fritz.
And that made me more or less active in finding a hiding place for-- for them.
I found a hiding place for the mother, the daughter in a grocery store.
I knew also members of our church in Haelen, about 30 kilometers from Maastricht.
And but for him, for Fritz I had nothing.
And his mother begged me to find something for [INAUDIBLE] him.
And I asked my mother, I says, OK that Fritz comes for a couple of days at our place until I find a place for him?
And my parents agreed to that.
So Fritz came to our place for three or four days.
And after that, my mom said, ah, he's such a nice fellow.
Let him stay here, and we don't have to go anymore.
And Fritz stayed at our place until the end of the war.
And through Fritz, I got more connections.
His-- his cousin was in the hospital.
And he was released from the hospital and needed a place.
I found also a place for her.
He brought me in contact with his uncle and his wife.
And slowly on, like a snowball, more and more people approached me for finding a hiding place.
It was-- it was 22 years of age, yeah.
I felt obliged to find a place for them.
Because the consequences were if you couldn't find a place, they would end up in camp, and might-- might be killed, or murdered, whatever.
It came from our vision in our church that we had to help those people who were in need.
And we had-- we always felt-- was very sympathetic to the Jewish people for the simple reason we studied the Old Testament.
So we know that they were people of the covenant, and that they [INAUDIBLE].
Through my religious upbringing, yes.
Christians who help people who are in need.
Yes.
That's correct.
Yeah.
No.
He had to stay at the home.
He couldn't go outside at all.
We had open houses.
Lots of people drop in at our place.
And we want to keep-- to keep on doing it.
Otherwise people are going to be suspicious.
That's right.
They were sitting [? with ?] his games together at night, you know.
And yeah, whatever.
He was the same age as I was.
Yeah.
22 years.
Right.
Yeah.
Well, when I asked first my mother, and she said, I have to discuss with Father.
And then she said, well, it's only for a few days.
We don't mind.
But after the course [INAUDIBLE] Fritz [INAUDIBLE].
So it wasn't a problem anymore.
And they--
Very nice guy, who was very flexible and had no problem adjusting to our family lifestyle.
So he peeled for my mom the potatoes.
He did the dish washing, and so on, and so on.
Yeah.
Majority worked out, yes.
And no problems there.
There was sometimes there was stress.
And it didn't click.
And then you had to look for a different place.
But the majority I had no problems with.
No.
Oh, yeah.
You know, some personalities you are not easygoing, you know.
Some-- some people want to-- didn't want to adjust to the lifestyle when the people were in the house where they were.
You know, that is the problem.
Everyone concerned.
I mean, it is not only with Jewish people.
That happens with every family that moves together in one house.
You know, you always get some stress and friction, yeah.
That's right.
I-- it was my-- I used to visit them once a month to deliver the-- the food stamps.
And then we sometimes picked up some mail for to be in contact with the family.
And if they had some problems, they asked me.
They say, well, is it possible that you find a different place for him?
Because we cannot get along.
But it didn't happen that often.
So.
Usually, if I could find a place, then I did that.
Yes.
No.
No, not at all.
I mean it's quite [INAUDIBLE] in the event for people who have-- well-to-do people who live in a big house, and all of a sudden they had to go in a small, [INAUDIBLE] hiding place.
That gives stress.
Oh yes.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
No.
I didn't know them them.
I didn't know them then.
All strangers to me, yes.
No.
No.
No.
I set up a scheme to have those food stamps.
First, I started Fritz.
We have a master card-- a master ID card for food stamps.
We got those already before the war set up with Dutch Goldman in case a war was coming.
And then the Master Card you had to go to a local office-- usually in schools or community halls.
And you had to take the master card along.
And he put a little stamp in the master card.
And on the stamp you got a sheet with food stamps for a whole month.
Now I, as you know in Holland, is-- everybody had to go, if he change his address, to City Hall.
And it's very strict.
If he don't do that, then you get fined with that.
So what I did, I checked and looked at how City Hall did that.
And the only date in the master card, he changed the address, and put a [INAUDIBLE] behind, it and that was it.
No stamp.
So I did it with Fritz myself.
I changed his address to not my address, but another address, and changed in my neighborhood, and put [INAUDIBLE] behind it.
And then I tried it out.
I went to one of these bureaus, and yeah, yeah, I got them, sheets, food stamps for him as well.
Then I did it also for his mother, and for his sister, and for his cousin.
And slowly on.
And a whole bunch of master cards.
But I couldn't have been to one office.
So he went from one to the other.
Two there, and then three there, you know.
And in that way.
Because if they didn't have food stamps, it was hard for the people where you're were hiding.
They had to go to the black market and pay big amounts of money for that.
And that way they could come the normal market, buy food.
It was very dangerous, of course.
I-- I had to-- it was a kind of risk I took.
But I had no problems with that.
Beginning it was only a few.
And then, as people knew that I found a source to find food stamps-- food stamps-- I got a whole bunch of cards, and ended up with more than 100.
And then it became dangerous.
So I found somebody in, you know, head office in Haelan.
I found somebody that was willing to do that at night, change it over.
And that was-- and then after that, they grow again.
And in the end, before he was arrested.
And there were 300 people who looked out to the food stamps.
Well you could-- they could have arrested me, or they could search me in the train, or whatever.
They could find those.
And they-- at the least he could have said, you're on the black market.
And he could arrest me for that.
I continued to do that, until the man in the head office, from the office in Haelen, got scared, too.
And then he asked me the next time when I visit him again, he said, I handed it over to a police officer.
And there was inspector of the police force in Haelen.
And he took care of that every month.
I had to go to his head bureau, picked up the food stamps, and hand them out to all the people.
No.
No.
Was there a time when the money that you used to-- to do what you were doing ran short?
That's right.
Yes.
That's right.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
Well, usually I went to the minister of our church in-- in a place somewhere in the southern part of Holland, and I ask him if he knew anybody in his congregation who are willing to hide Jews.
And a couple days later I visit him again.
And he introduced me to some-- two family who were willing to do that.
And they could do that, of course, because it was known that in our church, you know, they were all against national [? socialism. ?] So we end up going there.
That's right.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I just told you.
Through the food stamps.
And I-- [INAUDIBLE] brought us the sheet every month to these-- to these people.
And they could just at the normal market buy-- buy food and whatever was available.
That's right.
Yes.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
No, no.
The people who were hiding them, they bought the food.
They-- they couldn't go outside.
No.
I have never heard about that.
It might be, but I don't think so.
No, no, no, no.
No.
Only a small group.
A small group, they knew about it.
But the majority didn't know that, no.
Well it was member of our church, and people I have contact with through an illegal magazine.
I was a representative for that.
So I handed those people already beforehand 20 or 50 copies of that-- of that magazine, and got to know them, that they were-- I could trust them.
And then through them I could find other places.
Yeah.
First of all, like I say, I was using a different name, the name of Alexander.
I usually told people that they were not living in my state, or even in another city.
A couple times I had to transport a Jewish person-- persons from the railroad station to-- to my house for a couple of nights.
And then when they walked from the railroad station to my house, instead of the north-- taking the normal normal route-- I took them and go all different route.
And so that they could hardly could find a way back-- way back-- in case something would happen.
Yes, of course.
They had to have-- they had to have also an ID card, because you couldn't use their own ID card with a J on it.
So we had to falsified stolen ID cards.
And then a friend who was a student in high school, he pick-pocket coats from girls and men.
They usually had the ID card in their pocket.
Because it was easier for the people to go to the police officer and say something stole my ID card, and you were honest about it.
And that way we had somebody at the police office who was an artist.
And he changed the picture and copied the stamp exactly like it was before.
And they used that for the time being to identify themselves.
I heard my sister had problems when I was already arrested, that she got a note in the mail.
And the note said, we know you have a Jewish boy at home.
And we warn you.
And then they took Fritz away for a couple of-- couple of weeks-- to a friend of mine.
And then he came back, and nothing happened.
It was a warning.
But we had-- later on we got a feeling that it was somebody who was-- want to protect us.
No.
Not at all.
No.
The-- the people who were when they were in hiding, they used to go to the [NON-ENGLISH], family doctor.
I usually could trust him.
So there was no problem there.
The people couldn't go.
The doctor came at home.
Made house calls.
Yes.
Yeah.
That's right.
Yes.
No.
I know one occasion the Jewish lady was pregnant, and she got a baby at home.
And you could cover it up with the doctor.
So.
Yeah.
That's right.
Yeah.
I was a member originally from the organization Free Netherlands, to which [INAUDIBLE] the magazine.
But for helping Jewish and other people who were been brought into hiding, it was my own set up.
Later on, of course, we borrowed in-- into a large organization in Netherlands called LO.
And there we got more financial support, and we got more food stamps, yes, through them.
It's in the National Organization of Helping People in Hiding, yeah.
And it was a broad organization.
It was not only-- not only for Jewish, but all the political prisoners who had to go in hiding.
And boys who had to go to work in Germany, they are also hiding, yeah.
Yes.
That's right.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That's right.
Yeah.
Did you receive any assistance from the resistance group, or-- or anyone else in doing your work?
My sister helped me with doing some work for the-- for hiding Jews.
And some friends [INAUDIBLE] knows about it.
Which sister are you talking about?
Marg-- Margareta.
Margareta.
My younger sister, yeah.
I had a friend named John Bosch, who found places for me for Jewish children.
And he extended that quite a bit.
So yeah.
Yes.
It's John Bosch, B-O-S-C-H.
He was just last year honored at Yad Vashem.
Well, later on they-- they became more organized.
And they were involved in burglarizing the offices for food stamps.
They attacked sometimes German soldiers, or killed traitors.
Well, one-- you know, one brought in another friend, slowly on.
You know, you treat it like a snowball, until you get more people you can trust, and you know who they stand for.
That's right.
Yes.
Well, first of all, we didn't-- we didn't-- usually we had a-- a piece of paper, and ripped that in two pieces, and gave one guy one of the piece, and then the man is coming, we show the other piece.
He can trust him.
That way we introduce our self, usually.
No, no, no, no.
That's right.
As far as I know.
In the northern part of Holland more of those things happened.
But that's only thing I am aware of.
Yeah.
No.
I was already in prison at that time.
Yeah.
Members of the church, yes.
Well, the minister would approach some people that he thought, well, they might have a bigger house.
They can easily have some people in hiding.
And that way he informed me about it.
And then I visit those people, told them about the dangers, and all that.
You know.
And-- but they usually agreed to that.
Yeah.
That's correct.
First of all, I did it on the way, as a told you, by pick-pocketing people, and exchanging that.
But later on, there were [? complete ?] printing officers in Amsterdam.
And I got connection with them.
And then he copied the original ID card, and make it exactly the way it looked before.
And that way, even better of course.
Because the-- the stolen ones, they came on a big list.
And the numbers on the list that they are stolen.
And you're lucky there were no computers at that time.
Because it could take a long time before they found out what number it was.
Yeah.
I was in the-- well, for several years a traveling salesman.
First I worked in-- for a chemical company.
And I sold [INAUDIBLE] tea, and coffee, and those things to stores.
When I was finished was when I, you know, I have nothing to do anyone.
Then I joining a company for who sold office machines.
So it was known that I was a traveling salesman.
And when I quit there, because my work for hiding people was too much, I still carry on that name, and people interacted [? train ?] with me-- [? with ?] me.
And [? a sample ?] case, and so on and so on.
You just actively watch [INAUDIBLE] when you're working.
That's right.
Yes.
Well, I discussed with the manager.
And I said, well, you have to make a choice.
Either you work full-time for us, or you're doing other things.
And I decided to quit, and to keep on working for helping people who were in hiding.
I gave up paying job.
That's right.
Yes.
Yeah.
Groceries.
We used girls for delivering food stamps, because girls are not arrested.
If you are a man at 18, 19, 20, they would pick you up and send you to full work in Germany.
So they were-- they can easy do something than the men are doing.
No.
That's right.
Yes.
Of course, for them there was no danger to be picked up.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yeah.
No problem.
They were quite willing, yes.
That's right.
Yes.
For example, I got-- I had conflict in Amsterdam.
And the family, they asked me-- two friend, two student-- if they could visit them.
And they had a little baby who was 12 days old.
And they asked me for a hiding place for the baby.
And then took a girl along with me, went back to Amsterdam, pick up the baby in a little basket.
And acting as a married couple we went back to Maastricht.
And the baby came to my parents' place.
And after a few days, my mom said-- my mom said, we like the baby so much.
We can stay here.
Stay there till the end of the war.
That's right.
Yes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Can stay as well.
A Jewish baby.
Yes.
My sister did.
And Fritz did part of duties.
He gave the bottle, and whatever that he could do, yeah.
No.
My sister bleached the baby's hair.
It was black.
And we told the neighborhood that one them, my aunt was-- had TB, and was in the hospital, and asked us to look after the baby till she was back out of the hospital.
That's correct.
Yes.
And then my sister walk around with her.
And it was like a normal baby.
With dyed hair.
Yes.
Well, when the train, and the-- the girl I had with me, and there was even a German soldier.
He looked at it for a cute baby.
And somebody on the train said it looked like me, the father.
And another said it looked like the girl.
It looked like the mother.
So.
That's right.
Yes.
Yeah.
No.
No, no, no.
I had [INAUDIBLE] was arrested.
My parents encouraged me to do that.
They never-- we were-- we were scared, of course.
[INAUDIBLE] just got normal.
But they encouraged me to do that.
So.
No.
Not at all.
No, no, no.
Well, of course you were in danger.
But they never-- they never had somebody who searched the house.
And they never had a suspicion that they were involved.
Only when I was interrogated by the Gestapo when I was arrested, he tried to blackmail me more or less to tell me that my father was also arrested.
And I was [INAUDIBLE] for that, because I took my own responsibility for that, my parents.
And he said, your father's arrested, he said.
And he said he's in same prison where I was.
And then I-- a couple of days later, because I was very afraid of that, a couple days later I got a parcel through the Red Cross from home.
And the handwriting of the parcel is my father.
And that way I could play with the Germans, and acted if it was-- that I believed that.
But I knew it was not true.
OK.
